name: Leafy Greens
description: Spinach, kale, and other dark greens, raw or lightly sautéed.
whyRecommended: Very low in carbs and calories while packed with fiber and magnesium, so they barely move glucose at all.

name: Avocado
description: Creamy, mild fruit great sliced, mashed, or diced into salads.
whyRecommended: Mostly healthy monounsaturated fat and fiber with almost no sugar, which helps slow digestion in the whole meal.

name: Plain Greek Yogurt
description: Thick, tangy yogurt — best unsweetened, topped with your own fruit.
whyRecommended: High in protein and low in carbs, it keeps you full and causes a much smaller glucose rise than flavored yogurts.

name: Chia Seeds
description: Tiny seeds that swell in liquid — stir into yogurt, oats, or water.
whyRecommended: Almost entirely fiber, so they slow down how quickly nearby carbs are absorbed into the bloodstream.

name: Salmon
description: Fatty fish, grilled or baked, rich in omega-3s.
whyRecommended: Contains no carbohydrates and is rich in omega-3 fats, which support heart health — a common concern alongside diabetes.

name: Eggs
description: Versatile protein — boiled, scrambled, or as an omelet.
whyRecommended: A carb-free protein source that keeps you satisfied and steady, especially at breakfast when glucose is more sensitive.

name: Almonds
description: A handful of raw or lightly roasted almonds.
whyRecommended: Nearly carb-free with fiber, protein, and fat, making them a steady snack that won't spike glucose.

name: Broccoli
whyRecommended: Low in carbs and high in fiber and vitamin C, with a minimal effect on blood sugar.

name: Chickpeas
description: Cooked chickpeas, great in salads, stews, or roasted as a snack.
whyRecommended: A fiber-rich legume that digests slowly, giving a gentle, gradual rise in glucose rather than a spike.

name: Lentils
description: Cooked lentils in soups, salads, or as a side dish.
whyRecommended: High in fiber and plant protein, lentils are digested slowly and support steady energy levels.

name: Steel-Cut Oats
description: Hearty morning oats, cooked plain and topped with berries.
whyRecommended: Less processed than instant oats, so they break down more slowly and produce a gentler glucose response.

name: Quinoa
description: A fluffy, protein-rich grain that works in place of rice.
whyRecommended: Higher in protein and fiber than white rice, which helps moderate its impact on blood sugar.

name: Sweet Potato
description: Roasted or boiled sweet potato, skin on for extra fiber.
whyRecommended: Naturally sweet with more fiber and nutrients than a regular potato, so it's a smarter starchy swap in moderate portions.

name: Apple
description: A whole apple with the skin on, not juiced.
whyRecommended: The fiber in whole fruit slows sugar absorption dramatically compared to apple juice or dried apple.

name: Mixed Berries
description: Blueberries, strawberries, and raspberries — fresh or frozen.
whyRecommended: Lower in sugar than most fruits and high in fiber and antioxidants, making them a gentler sweet treat.

name: Cinnamon
description: A spice to sprinkle on oats, yogurt, or coffee.
whyRecommended: Adds warmth and sweetness without any carbs, and some studies suggest it may modestly support insulin sensitivity.
